response:
To find the equilibrium population size we set the rate of change to zero meaning dPdt equals zero. The equation becomes rP times one minus P over K equals h(t). With r equal to 0.1 and K equal to 1000 and h(t) equal to 10 plus 5 times sine of pi t over 6 we solve for P. This yields a quadratic equation where the constant term depends on the sine function. The equilibrium P_e is the positive root of the equation 0.1P minus 0.0001P squared equals 10 plus 5 sine of pi t over 6. Rearranging gives 0.0001P squared minus 0.1P plus 10 plus 5 sine of pi t over 6 equals zero. Using the quadratic formula we find P_e equals 500 plus or minus the square root of 250000 minus 100 times 10 plus 5 sine of pi t over 6. Since the population must be positive and less than K we take the smaller root which is 500 minus the square root of 250000 minus 1000 minus 500 sine of pi t over 6. This simplifies to 500 minus the square root of 249000 minus 500 sine of pi t over 6.

When the carrying capacity changes to K(t) equal to 1000 minus 3t the equilibrium becomes time dependent. The new equation is 0.1P times one minus P over 1000 minus 3t equals 10 plus 5 sine of pi t over 6. The stability of this equilibrium depends on the sign of the derivative of the growth function with respect to P. As K decreases the equilibrium population shrinks. The population becomes unsustainable when the equilibrium value drops to zero or below. This happens when the maximum possible growth cannot offset the harvesting. The critical moment occurs when the discriminant of the quadratic equation becomes negative or when the positive root vanishes. For the population to exist we need the term under the square root to be non negative and the resulting P to be positive. The condition for extinction is when the harvesting rate exceeds the maximum sustainable yield. The maximum yield for a logistic model is rK over 4. Here rK over 4 equals 0.1 times
